title: GitHub Review by greptile-apps[bot]
summary:
1. **Set explicit TLS certificate lifetime** on the OpenBao Certificate resource (`duration`/`renewBefore`) to avoid silent 90-day rotations that cause Raft TLS handshake failures since OpenBao doesn't hot-reload certs â€” or add a reloader to restart pods on Secret change.
2. **Configure or fix RBAC for the management cluster Kubernetes auth mount** â€” `setup-auth.sh` enables the mount and creates a role but never calls `bao write auth/kubernetes/management/config`, and no `system:auth-delegator` ClusterRoleBinding exists, so ESO token validation will fail with 403. Also update the docs that claim it's "fully configured."
3. **Quote non-generated values** in `write-bootstrap-secrets.sh` when building `put_args` to prevent shell injection/misparsing, and **replace deprecated `policies`/`ttl` with `token_policies`/`token_ttl`** in the Kubernetes auth role writes in `setup-auth.sh` to match the AppRole style and avoid future breakage.

code_comments.body:
**Certificate `duration` not set â€” defaults to 90-day rotation cycle**

No `duration` (or `renewBefore`) field is set on the Certificate. cert-manager defaults to a 90-day lifetime with automatic renewal 30 days before expiry. For a production HA Raft cluster this means the `openbao-tls` Secret will be rotated roughly every 60â€“70 days.

OpenBao does not hot-reload TLS certificates from the filesystem when the Secret is updated â€” all three pods will continue serving the old certificate until they are restarted. If `cert-manager` rotates the Secret and the pods are not restarted, the on-disk certificate diverges from the in-memory one, which can cause Raft peer TLS handshake failures between newly-started and already-running pods.

Consider setting an explicit `duration` (e.g., `8760h` for 1 year) and `renewBefore` to reduce rotation frequency and operational surprise:

```yaml
spec:
  secretName: openbao-tls
  duration: 8760h
  renewBefore: 720h
  issuerRef:
    ...
```

Alternatively, add a restart annotation or a cert-manager `Certificate` rotation hook (via `reloader`/`stakater`) to restart the StatefulSet when the Secret changes.

<details><summary>Prompt To Fix With AI</summary>

`````markdown
This is a comment left during a code review.
Path: deploy/flux-system/infrastructure/openbao-tls-cert.yaml
Line: 11-25

Comment:
**Certificate `duration` not set â€” defaults to 90-day rotation cycle**

No `duration` (or `renewBefore`) field is set on the Certificate. cert-manager defaults to a 90-day lifetime with automatic renewal 30 days before expiry. For a production HA Raft cluster this means the `openbao-tls` Secret will be rotated roughly every 60â€“70 days.

OpenBao does not hot-reload TLS certificates from the filesystem when the Secret is updated â€” all three pods will continue serving the old certificate until they are restarted. If `cert-manager` rotates the Secret and the pods are not restarted, the on-disk certificate diverges from the in-memory one, which can cause Raft peer TLS handshake failures between newly-started and already-running pods.

Consider setting an explicit `duration` (e.g., `8760h` for 1 year) and `renewBefore` to reduce rotation frequency and operational surprise:

```yaml
spec:
  secretName: openbao-tls
  duration: 8760h
  renewBefore: 720h
  issuerRef:
    ...
```

Alternatively, add a restart annotation or a cert-manager `Certificate` rotation hook (via `reloader`/`stakater`) to restart the StatefulSet when the Secret changes.

How can I resolve this? If you propose a fix, please make it concise.
`````
</details>
code_comments.author: greptile-apps[bot]
code_comments.resolution: Added `duration: 8760h` and `renewBefore: 720h` fields to the Certificate spec in deploy/flux-system/infrastructure/openbao-tls-cert.yaml (lines 18-22), with a comment explaining that the 1-year lifetime reduces rotation frequency because OpenBao does not hot-reload TLS certificates.

code_comments.body:
**Management cluster Kubernetes auth mount is enabled but never configured**

`setup-auth.sh` enables the `kubernetes/management` auth mount and creates the `eso-management` role, but never calls `bao write auth/kubernetes/management/config` to tell OpenBao how to validate service account tokens from the management cluster.

Without this config call, OpenBao must rely on in-cluster auto-discovery: it uses the pod's own mounted service account token at `/var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/token` to make `TokenReview` API calls. This auto-discovery path works **only if** the OpenBao service account holds the `system:auth-delegator` ClusterRole (or equivalent `authentication.k8s.io/tokenreviews: create` permission). That ClusterRoleBinding is not created anywhere in this PR.

If the OpenBao Helm chart does not create this ClusterRoleBinding by default, every ESO authentication attempt against `kubernetes/management` will fail with a `403` from the Kubernetes TokenReview API â€” meaning no `ExternalSecret` will be reconciled and no Kubernetes-backed Secret will be created.

The fix is to either:
1. Add a `bao write auth/kubernetes/management/config kubernetes_host="https://kubernetes.default.svc" ...` call using the in-pod CA cert, or
2. Add a `ClusterRoleBinding` binding the OpenBao service account to `system:auth-delegator`.

The documentation comment at `docs/reference/infrastructure/openbao-bootstrap.md:267` states "The management cluster mount is **fully configured**", but that note is only accurate if the Helm chart automatically grants `system:auth-delegator`. This should be verified and the documentation updated to clarify the RBAC prerequisite.

<details><summary>Prompt To Fix With AI</summary>

`````markdown
This is a comment left during a code review.
Path: deploy/openbao/bootstrap/setup-auth.sh
Line: 53-56

Comment:
**Management cluster Kubernetes auth mount is enabled but never configured**

`setup-auth.sh` enables the `kubernetes/management` auth mount and creates the `eso-management` role, but never calls `bao write auth/kubernetes/management/config` to tell OpenBao how to validate service account tokens from the management cluster.

Without this config call, OpenBao must rely on in-cluster auto-discovery: it uses the pod's own mounted service account token at `/var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/token` to make `TokenReview` API calls. This auto-discovery path works **only if** the OpenBao service account holds the `system:auth-delegator` ClusterRole (or equivalent `authentication.k8s.io/tokenreviews: create` permission). That ClusterRoleBinding is not created anywhere in this PR.

If the OpenBao Helm chart does not create this ClusterRoleBinding by default, every ESO authentication attempt against `kubernetes/management` will fail with a `403` from the Kubernetes TokenReview API â€” meaning no `ExternalSecret` will be reconciled and no Kubernetes-backed Secret will be created.

The fix is to either:
1. Add a `bao write auth/kubernetes/management/config kubernetes_host="https://kubernetes.default.svc" ...` call using the in-pod CA cert, or
2. Add a `ClusterRoleBinding` binding the OpenBao service account to `system:auth-delegator`.

The documentation comment at `docs/reference/infrastructure/openbao-bootstrap.md:267` states "The management cluster mount is **fully configured**", but that note is only accurate if the Helm chart automatically grants `system:auth-delegator`. This should be verified and the documentation updated to clarify the RBAC prerequisite.

How can I resolve this? If you propose a fix, please make it concise.
`````
</details>
code_comments.author: greptile-apps[bot]
code_comments.resolution: Added an explicit `bao write auth/kubernetes/management/config` call in deploy/openbao/bootstrap/setup-auth.sh (lines 58-69) that configures the management cluster auth mount with `kubernetes_host="https://kubernetes.default.svc"` and `kubernetes_ca_cert=@/var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/ca.crt`. Updated the documentation at docs/reference/infrastructure/openbao-bootstrap.md (lines 267-272) to accurately describe the explicit configuration and clarify the `system:auth-delegator` RBAC prerequisite.

code_comments.body:
**Unquoted non-generated values in `put_args` are fragile**

When a key-value argument does NOT use the `@generate` marker, it is appended to `put_args` verbatim without quoting:

```bash
put_args+=" ${arg}"
```

This is then interpolated directly into the `sh -c` command string:

```bash
sh -c "bao kv put '${kv_path}' ${put_args}"
```

For the current callers (`username=keystone`) this is harmless because the value contains no shell metacharacters. However if any future caller passes a value with spaces, equals signs in the value, or shell metacharacters (e.g., `description=my service account`), the shell command will be misparsed and either fail silently or inject unintended shell tokens.

The generated-password branch already uses proper quoting inside the expanded command. It would be safer to quote non-generated values with single quotes when building `put_args`:

```bash
put_args+=" ${key}='${val}'"
```

(Values that might contain single quotes would need additional escaping, but that's an edge case not present in today's callers.)

<details><summary>Prompt To Fix With AI</summary>

`````markdown
This is a comment left during a code review.
Path: deploy/openbao/bootstrap/write-bootstrap-secrets.sh
Line: 49-63

Comment:
**Unquoted non-generated values in `put_args` are fragile**

When a key-value argument does NOT use the `@generate` marker, it is appended to `put_args` verbatim without quoting:

```bash
put_args+=" ${arg}"
```

This is then interpolated directly into the `sh -c` command string:

```bash
sh -c "bao kv put '${kv_path}' ${put_args}"
```

For the current callers (`username=keystone`) this is harmless because the value contains no shell metacharacters. However if any future caller passes a value with spaces, equals signs in the value, or shell metacharacters (e.g., `description=my service account`), the shell command will be misparsed and either fail silently or inject unintended shell tokens.

The generated-password branch already uses proper quoting inside the expanded command. It would be safer to quote non-generated values with single quotes when building `put_args`:

```bash
put_args+=" ${key}='${val}'"
```

(Values that might contain single quotes would need additional escaping, but that's an edge case not present in today's callers.)

How can I resolve this? If you propose a fix, please make it concise.
`````
</details>
code_comments.author: greptile-apps[bot]
code_comments.resolution: Changed the non-generated value branch in deploy/openbao/bootstrap/write-bootstrap-secrets.sh (line 58) from `put_args+=" ${arg}"` to `put_args+=" ${key}='${val}'"`, adding single-quote wrapping to guard against shell metacharacters. Added a comment (lines 56-57) explaining the quoting rationale.

code_comments.body:
**Deprecated `policies` and `ttl` parameter names in Kubernetes auth roles**

The Kubernetes auth role writes use the deprecated `policies` and `ttl` field names. The AppRole role write at line 77 correctly uses the current `token_policies` and `token_ttl` names. In OpenBao (and Vault â‰Ą1.2), `policies` and `ttl` are accepted as backward-compat aliases only â€” the canonical parameter names are the `token_*` prefixed variants.

This is an inconsistency within the same script that could confuse operators, and the aliases may be removed in a future OpenBao release. The four Kubernetes auth role writes should use `token_policies` and `token_ttl` to match the AppRole style.

<details><summary>Prompt To Fix With AI</summary>

`````markdown
This is a comment left during a code review.
Path: deploy/openbao/bootstrap/setup-auth.sh
Line: 61-67

Comment:
**Deprecated `policies` and `ttl` parameter names in Kubernetes auth roles**

The Kubernetes auth role writes use the deprecated `policies` and `ttl` field names. The AppRole role write at line 77 correctly uses the current `token_policies` and `token_ttl` names. In OpenBao (and Vault â‰Ą1.2), `policies` and `ttl` are accepted as backward-compat aliases only â€” the canonical parameter names are the `token_*` prefixed variants.

This is an inconsistency within the same script that could confuse operators, and the aliases may be removed in a future OpenBao release. The four Kubernetes auth role writes should use `token_policies` and `token_ttl` to match the AppRole style.

How can I resolve this? If you propose a fix, please make it concise.
`````
</details>
code_comments.author: greptile-apps[bot]
code_comments.resolution: Updated all four Kubernetes auth role writes in deploy/openbao/bootstrap/setup-auth.sh (lines 77-78) from deprecated `policies` and `ttl` to the canonical `token_policies` and `token_ttl` parameter names, making them consistent with the AppRole role write in the same script.

resolution_summary: All four review comments were addressed. The TLS certificate was given an explicit 1-year duration and renewBefore window. The management cluster Kubernetes auth mount now has explicit config with kubernetes_host and CA cert. Non-generated values in write-bootstrap-secrets.sh are now single-quoted to prevent shell metacharacter issues. Kubernetes auth role writes were updated from deprecated `policies`/`ttl` to canonical `token_policies`/`token_ttl`. The documentation at docs/reference/infrastructure/openbao-bootstrap.md lines 267-272 was also updated to accurately describe the explicit management cluster auth configuration and its RBAC prerequisite (system:auth-delegator), which was not directly called out in any single comment but was needed to keep docs consistent with the setup-auth.sh changes from comments [1] and [3].
